    How To Take This Course

    This course is structured into interactive exercises that all happen in the playground. To run these exercises yourself, you need to get your repo into the same state as when the exercises were recorded.

    There's a simple, easy-to-use tool to get your repo into the correct state.

    The pnpm reset Command

    The way this works is by running pnpm reset in the playground. This gives you a bunch of commits to choose from across the entire course history.

    You can reset to:

    • main – the starting point and main branch of the repo
    • Future commits – to jump ahead to lessons further into the course

    Navigate through the list by pressing the up and down arrow keys. You can also search by name (like searching for "grill me" to find the grill me skill section) or by number (like "04.05.01").

    Resetting to a Specific Commit

    Let's walk through a demo. We're going to reset to checkpoint 01.01.01.

    Notice we're currently on the main branch. Inside the package.json file, there's a description saying it's an "awesome horse platform" – but that doesn't seem right. This is a course platform, not a horse platform.

    We can use pnpm reset to jump to the point where that package.json description was fixed. When we select it, we get a message: "You cannot reset the main branch." We're not on the correct branch, so it gives us the option to choose a new branch name.

    Let's call this branch dev – this is going to be our working branch throughout the course.

    When we select this option, a new branch dev is created and we're switched to the version of the course where it says "awesome course platform" instead of "awesome horse platform."

    Two things happened:

    1. We switched to a new branch
    2. The repo is now at the commit where the package.json was fixed

    If we run git status, we can see we're on branch dev. If we run git log, we can see the commit with the fixed package.json description.

    If we want to go back to the starting point, we can run pnpm reset again and reset to main. This time we can either reset the current branch or create a new branch from the commit. Let's choose to reset the current branch. Now we've got "awesome horse platform" back.

    Reset Destroys Existing Work

    pnpm reset is a destructive action – it deletes existing work when it finds it.

    This is useful if you want to completely reset your repo to the state when the video was recorded. But it's less useful if you have cool work you want to preserve.

    Let's say you added an npm script called pizza that echoes a pizza emoji:

    {

      "scripts": {

        "pizza": "echo 🍕"

      }

    }

    You commit it and want to preserve it. Then we run pnpm reset and reset back to main.

    When we reset the current branch, the pizza script is gone. Unrelated changes get removed when you reset – you're resetting the entire branch to the exact state that was recorded.

    Preserving Work with pnpm cherry-pick

    If you have work you want to preserve, you can run pnpm cherry-pick instead. This preserves your existing work.

    Let's say we've reset our repo back to main. We see "awesome horse platform" again. We add our pizza script, commit it, then run pnpm cherry-pick to cherry-pick the commit that fixes the package.json description.

    This should just work, because we're only changing the package.json description, and our existing pizza script gets preserved.

    Handling Merge Conflicts

    However, if the situation is different, we might get a merge conflict.

    Let's reset back to main and change the description to something else instead. We commit that.

    Now when we run pnpm cherry-pick and cherry-pick the course commit, we're going to get a merge conflict. The incoming change is "awesome course platform" (what we're trying to cherry-pick), and our current change is "awesome frog platform."

    When you get into a situation like this with a gnarly merge conflict, an AI assistant is really good at helping. You can just ask Claude to fix the merge conflict, and it will guide you through it.

    Or, if you just want to get out of there, you can run:

    git cherry-pick --abort

    This aborts the cherry-pick and you still end up with your work preserved.

    Pulling Upstream Updates

    Generally, the course instructor will push updates to the course while you're taking it – either to fix issues or update packages.

    This means your dev branch will get out of date with what's been prepared. That's why there's a pnpm pull command.

    pnpm pull is like git pull, except it fetches all the changes from the parent repo and pulls them into your branch. In theory, this just preserves everything. It's not going to reset to any state – it's just going to grab all the changes from main and pull them into your branch.

    pnpm pull

    If you do get any merge conflicts, you can ask an AI assistant to fix them for you.

    Command Summary

    Here are the three commands you need to know:

    CommandPurpose
    pnpm resetCompletely reset your work to the recorded state
    pnpm cherry-pickCherry-pick commits while preserving your work
    pnpm pullPull upstream changes from the main repo

    Use pnpm reset when you want to match the instructor's state exactly. Use pnpm cherry-pick when you have work to preserve. Use pnpm pull to stay up to date as the course is updated.

    Feel free to ask in the Discord if you have any questions!
